Data Structure Algorithm

Introduction
If you’re preparing for a coding interview or just brushing up on array manipulation, one of the most common tasks you’ll run into is finding the minimum, maximum, or top N elements in an array. This pattern shows up constantly — from simple “find the largest number in a list” interview questions to production code that needs to surface the top 5 best-selling products or the top 5 highest scores.
In this guide, we’ll walk through how to find the min, max, and top 5 elements in an array using both Java and Scala, comparing imperative and functional approaches so you can pick the style that fits your codebase.
Why This Problem Matters
Finding min/max/top-N values is a foundational building block used in:

• Coding interview questions (FAANG-style array problems)

• Leaderboard and ranking systems

• Data analytics pipelines (top 5 selling products, top 5 slowest API calls)

• Anomaly detection (spotting outliers via min/max)

• Real-time monitoring dashboards
